coredata.dc:title: A colorimetric indicator-displacement assay based on stable Cu2+ selective carbon dots for fluorescence turn-on detection of pyrophosphate anions in urine
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Yi
Chen, Pei
Liu, Yuqing
Cai, Zheng
Wang, Xiyao
Me, Yang
Ding, Xiongyu
Lin, Lan
Jiang, Huijun
Zhang, Zhenqin
Ju, Yichun
coredata.dc:description: Determination of PPi levels in urine represents a measurable factor for diagnostic, treatment, and monitoring of urolithiasis. Owing to the quenching ability of Cu2+ on fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) and strong binding affinity between Cu2+ and PPi, we develop a new off–on assay for PPi detection using newly BPHA CDs (BPHA: N,N-bis(pyridin-2-ylmethyl)hexan-1-amine). The fluorescence intensity of BPHA CDs was significantly quenched by Cu2+ (“off”) through forming BPHA CDs/Cu2+ complexes and the fluorescence intensity of BPHA CDs /Cu2+ system was completely resumed by PPi (“on”) owing to the release of free Cu2+. The fluorescence turn-off/on approach showed a highly selective response to PPi over the large family of other anions. The detection limits were 0.094 μM for Cu2+ and 0.025 μM for PPi, respectively. A wide linear range for PPi was up to 4400 μM. The indicator displacement assay (IDAs) using pyrocatechol violet (PV) as a colorimetric indicator was carried out to detect PPi with the naked eyes. The “off–on” fluorescent sensor based on BPHA CDs shows many merits, including convenient operation, cost-saving, high sensitivity, selectivity, stability and wide detecting range, which is applied to PPi detection in human urine sample.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Colorimetric
IDAs fluorescent
Copper (II) detection
Pyrophosphate

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection range and efficiency of PPi
Fluorescence responses comparison of CDs and BPHA CDs
Ppi binding performance on BPHA CDs by PV indicator

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) HRTEM of BPHA CDs; (B) 1H NMR of CDs and BPHA CDs in D2O; (C) UV–vis absorption (a), excitation (b), emission (c) spectra of BPHA CDs and UV–vis absorption of CDs (d); (D) Stability of BPHA CDs.

The fluorescence response of BPHA CDs upon the addition of different concentrations of Cu2+ at 25 °C. Insert: relative fluorescence intensity of BPHA CDs at 450 nm (F/F0) upon addition of different concentrations of Cu2+. λex is 340 nm.

Selectivity of BPHA CDs for Cu2+ (500 μM) over other metal ions (black bars) and the mixture of Cu2+ with other metal ions (red bars). The concentrations of Na+ and K+ are 1000 μM and concentrations of other cations are 500 μM. λex is 340 nm.
fluorescence spectra changes of the BPHA CDs-cations upon addition of Cu2+. The concentrations of Na+ and K+ are 1000 μM and concentrations of other cations are 500 μM.

The fluorescence response of the BPHA CDs/Cu2+ system upon the addition of different concentrations of PPi in 10 mM HEPES buffer (pH 7.4) at 25 °C. Insert: relative fluorescence intensity of BPHA CDs/Cu2+ at 450 nm (F/F0) upon addition of different concentrations of PPi. λex is 340 nm.
Selectivity of BPHA CDs/Cu2+ system for PPi(200 μM) over other anions(200 μM) (black bars) and the mixture of PPi with other anions (red bars). λex is 340 nm.
Fluorescence spectra changes of the BPHA CDs/Cu2+-anions upon addition of PPi. The concentrations of PPi are 200 μM and concentrations of other anions are 200 μM.
The fluorescence quench efficiency with the addition of [Cu2+] and the fluorescence recover efficiency with the addition of [PPi].
The fluorescence spectra of BPHA CDs under different conditions. The concentrations of Cu2+ and PPi are 1000 and 200 μM, respectively.
Changes in absorbance for PV (20.0 μM) upon addition of BPHA CDs/Cu2+. Insert: fitting curves at 600 nm.

Colors of the BPHA CDs with or without the addition of Cu2+ (20 μM), PV (20 μM) or PPi (200 μM). From left to right: BPHA CDs, BPHA CDs + Cu2+, Cu2+, BPHA CDs/ Cu2+ + PPi, BPHA CDs/Cu2+ + PV, PV, BPHA CDs + PV, BPHA CDs/Cu2+/PV + PPi, BPHA CDs/PV + PPi.
Changes in absorbance for BPHA CDs/Cu2+(20 μM)/PV(20 μM) upon addition of PPi. All spectra were measured in 10 mM HEPES buffer (pH 7.4) at 25 °C. Insert: fitting curves at 600 nm.
Schematic illustration of synthetic procedures for BPHA CDs and the application for Cu2+ and PPi detection.
Results of PPi detection in human urine using BPHA/Cu2+/PV system.

head.ce:title: A colorimetric indicator-displacement assay based on stable Cu2+ selective carbon dots for fluorescence turn-on detection of pyrophosphate anions in urine

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Determination of PPi levels in urine represents a measurable factor for diagnostic, treatment, and monitoring of urolithiasis. Owing to the quenching ability of Cu2+ on fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) and strong binding affinity between Cu2+ and PPi, we develop a new off–on assay for PPi detection using newly BPHA CDs (BPHA: N,N-bis(pyridin-2-ylmethyl)hexan-1-amine). The fluorescence intensity of BPHA CDs was significantly quenched by Cu2+ (“off”) through forming BPHA CDs/Cu2+ complexes and the fluorescence intensity of BPHA CDs /Cu2+ system was completely resumed by PPi (“on”) owing to the release of free Cu2+. The fluorescence turn-off/on approach showed a highly selective response to PPi over the large family of other anions. The detection limits were 0.094 μM for Cu2+ and 0.025 μM for PPi, respectively. A wide linear range for PPi was up to 4400 μM. The indicator displacement assay (IDAs) using pyrocatechol violet (PV) as a colorimetric indicator was carried out to detect PPi with the naked eyes. The “off–on” fluorescent sensor based on BPHA CDs shows many merits, including convenient operation, cost-saving, high sensitivity, selectivity, stability and wide detecting range, which is applied to PPi detection in human urine sample.

ce:section.ce:para: In industrialized community, urolithiasis is one of the most common urologic diseases with the occurrence of 15% to 25% by various stone types [1,2]. About 75% of urine stones are principally made of calcium oxalate, of which up to 50% is calcium hydroxyl phosphate [3,4]. Pyrophosphate (P2O7 4–, PPi) is a by-product of cellular hydrolysis of ATP, which plays a crucial role in energy storage, signal transmission and important cellular metabolic process [5,6]. More importantly, PPi can work as a natural inhibitor of urolithiasis by inhibiting the formation of the above calcium-containing crystals in the urinary tract. In fact, the presence of 16 to 40 μM of sodium pyrophosphate in urine is enough to inhibit the formation of calcium oxalate and calcium phosphate crystals [7]. Thus, determination of PPi levels in urine for diagnostic, treatment, and monitoring of urolithiasis has come into focus.

In recent decades, considerable studies based on traditional measurement methods such as fluorescence method [8-13], enzymatic techniques [14-16], colorimetric method [17,18], electrochemical method [19,20], chemiluminescence [21], bioluminescence [22,23], and organic sensors [24-26], have been developed for PPi detection. Among mentioned studies, the fluorescent method has received intensive attention owing to its high selectivity, sensitivity and simplicity [27]. For example, Ghosh’s group developed a small organic molecular system composed of a hydroxy-naphthaldehyde unit covalently connected with 9, 10-phenanthrene imidazole moiety, which can act as an extremely sensitive ratiometric fluorescence sensor for PPi in solution [28]. Besides easy and low cost synthesis, ideal probes for PPi should have the merits of high solubility, sensitivity, selectivity and easy applicability for practical purposes without other interference. Fluorescent probes for Cu2+ and PPi are especially emerging as one of the most successful strategies because of their simplicity and efficiency [8,29-33]. However, few studies have been reported on fluorescent sensors for PPi in urine up to now [8-12,34-37]. It is believed that issues such as convenient fluorescent sensors with naked-eye sensing in 100% aqueous solution over inorganic/organic phosphates remain unsolved in the fields of urinary PPi detection [34].

Indicator displacement assay (IDAs) is one of the well-known strategies for anion sensing [38]. In a typical IDAs, a reversible bond is first formed between an indicator and a receptor in both organic and aqueous media. Then, a competitive analyte introduced into the system breaks the bond and displac the receptor with the indicator, which in turn modulates an accompanying colorimetric signal and visible color change by naked eyes [39,40]. IDAs possesses many advantages such as simple synthesis, the fact that numerous dyes can be used for one type of receptor and wild availability of indicators [41]. Recently, it has been reported that IDAs has been successfully applied to sense a variety of analytes such as t, t-muconic acid, carboxylic acids, cysteine and histidine in urine [42-45]. However, IDAs has not yet been applied to urinary PPi detection.

In contribution to our efforts to develop highly sensitive chemical and biological sensors [46], herein we applied the colorimetric IDAs approach in a BPHA CDs for simple and efficient fluorescence turn-on detection of PPi in urine. The design strategy of BPHA CDs is shown in Scheme 1 . In this colorimetric IDAs approach, a commercially available dye, pyrocatechol violet (PV) was used as the indicator and the selective detection of PPi was achieved by taking advantage of the PPi-Cu2+ affinity pair. The design of the IDAs-based fluorescence turn-on sensor, optimization of main factors, evaluation of the performance, and application to analysis of PPi in human urine were studied in detail.

All of the solvents used in sensor preparation were of analytic grade and all chemicals were used as received unless otherwise noted. BPHA was prepared by the reported methods [47]. Copper nitrate (Cu(NO3)2 ⋅ 3H2O), NH4HCO3, N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N’-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and other inorganic salts used were purchased from Sino-pharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium pyrophosphate and ethylenediamine were purchased from J & K. 2-Morpholinoethanesulfonic acid (MES) was purchase from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). The purified water obtained from Millipore (greater than18.2 MΩ) was throughout all the experiments. HEPES buffer solution (10 mM, pH = 7.4) was used in all experiments.

FT-IR spectra were recorded on a Tensor-27 FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) with a resolution of 2 cm-1 and a spectral range of 4000–400 cm-1.The fluorescence spectra were recorded with an F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra were measured with a UV-2450 UV–vis spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images were acquired for the estimation of average size of CDs on a JEOL JEM-2100 (HR) transmission electron microscopy (Tokyo, Japan). The 1H NMR spectra were recorded on a JEOL 400 with TMS as the internal reference. All pH measurements of media were accomplished using a Model PHS-3C meter.

0.2 g sodium citrate and 1.5 g NH4HCO3 were dissolved in 10 mL purified water and the mixture was added into a reaction kettle and heated at 180 °C for 4 h. After the kettle was cooled to ambient temperature naturally, the CDs were filtered using a 0.2 μm syringe for subsequent experiments.

To activate CDs, 0.5 mL CDs (50 mg) , 100 μL EDC, 100 μL NHS (1 mol L-1) and 10 mL MES buffer (pH = 6.0) were added into a round-bottom flask, and stirred for 30 min at ambient temperature. Solution of 62.27 mg BPHA in 3.5 mL methanol and 3.5 mL ethanol was added. Then the mixture was stirred at 25 °C for 4 h. Before dialyzing, methanol and ethanol were removed by rotate evaporation. After that, the solution containing the BPHA CDs was dialyzed for 10 h using dialysis membrane of 1000 MWCO to removal of excess reactants (change 2000 mL purified water every two hours) and freeze-dried. Finally, the BPHA CDs were dispersed in 10 mM HEPES buffer (pH 7.4) at a concentration of 15.52 μg mL−1 for further application.

For the detection of Cu2+, CDs solution and different amounts of Cu2+ were added into a 2.0 mL centrifuge tube, and the mixture was diluted to 1.5 mL with purified water and mixed cations were chosen to assess the influence of co-existing ions and evaluate the selectivity for Cu2+. For the detection of PPi, CDs solution, Cu2+ (0.1 mM) and then different amounts of PPi were added into a 2.0 mL centrifuge tube, next the mixture was diluted to 1.5 mL with purified water and fully mixed. Anions were chosen to assess the influence of co-existing ions on the fluorescence of CDs/Cu2+ system and evaluate the selectivity for PPi. The human urine sample was pretreated before use [36]. Briefly, the urine sample (20 mL) was added by an aqueous HCl solution (200 μL, 6 M), and then the mixture was purified by multi-walled carbon nanotubes (40 mg). Next, the solution of HClO4 (320 μL, 1 M), ammonium molybdate (80 μL, 0.08 M), and triethylamine hydrochloride (160 μL, 0.1 M, pH 5.0) were successively added to the purified sample (2 mL). After a clear supernatant was obtained by twice centrifugation (10,000 rpm, 20 min) of the mixture, the metal ions such as Mg2+, Fe2+, and Fe3+ in urine were precipitated by NaOH. At last, HCl was used to adjust the pH of supernatant to 7.0. For PPi assay in human urine, 1.5 mL BPHA CDs (15.52 μg mL−1, dispersed in 10 mM HEPES) was mixed with 100 μL Cu2+ (400 μM), then 100 μL PV (400 μM) was added to the above mixture to obtain the BPHA CDs/Cu2+/PV system. The pre-treated urine sample (200 μL) was mixed with HEPES buffer or aqueous Na4P2O7 solution (100 μL) with a certain concentration, the absorbance of which was recorded by adding the BPHA CDs/Cu2+/PV system. In addition, the fluorescence spectra were collected at room temperature subsequently from 353 nm to 580 nm with excitation wavelength at 340 nm, and the slit widths of the excitation and emission were both 5 nm.

BPHA CDs was synthesized in high yield and purity by one step reaction between CDs and BPHA following a simple purification procedure. The morphologies of BPHA CDs was characterized by a high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). After BPHA was linked, the product was extensively washed with large amounts of water and collected through dialysis. BPHA CDs with monodispersed with diameter ~ 10 nm were obtained (Fig. 1 A).

As Fig. 1B shown, 1H NMR was used to confirm the linage of BPHA to CDs. In comparison with proton signals of CDs, distinct downfield shifting over the range 7.75–6.75 ppm corresponding to aromatic protons of the pyridine moieties were also observed in BPHA CDs. Moreover, single peak at 4.19 ppm can be assigned protons of pyridine methylene and upfield shifting over the range 3.3–3.25, 2.3–2.1 and 2.0–1.9 can be assigned protons of methylene.

UV–vis absorption (a), excitation (b) and emission (c) spectra were recorded to investigate the optical properties of BPHA CDs. As Fig. 1C shows, BPHA CDs have absorption peaks at 336 nm and 259 nm, which indicates the n–π* transition of C = O bonds and the π–π* transition of C = C bonds, respectively [48]. The maximum excitation wavelength was recorded to be 340 nm, which is related to the rigidity of the non-conjugated structure [49,50]. Meanwhile, the maximum emission wavelength of BPHA CDs was recorded to be 450 nm. A full width at half maximum of BPHA CDs was around 65 nm.

Furthermore, the stability of BPHA CDs was investigated. There was no observation of precipitated or floating particles even after being kept for 10 days, and the fluorescent intensity of BPHA CDs displayed a negligible change, indicating that BPHA CDs exhibited good fluorescent stability. (See Fig. 1D)

Fig. S1 showed the FT-IR spectrum of BPHA CDs. The absorption peaks at about 3419 cm−1 and 1399 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration and in-plane bending vibration of –NH, respectively. The peak at 1594 cm−1 was associated with asymmetric stretching vibration of the carboxylate anions or the bending vibration of N–H [51]. The characteristic peaks at 1648 cm−1 and 1043 cm−1 could be attributed to the stretching vibration of C = O. The absorption peaks at about 2941 cm−1 and 1566 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibration of C–H bonds and aromatic ring, respectively. The characteristic peak at 870 cm−1 was out-plane bending vibration of C–H in pyridine.
The parameters including media pH, concentrations of NaCl and BPHA CDs concentration were optimized.

Due to the sensitivity of CDs to pH change, the effects of pH on the fluorescence intensity of BPHA CDs were investigated in the range of 1.33 to 13.33. As shown in Fig. S2, the fluorescence intensity of BPHA CDs at 450 nm increased with the pH value in the range of 1.33–7.37, and dropped slightly until the pH value of 13.33, which demonstrates similar trends with CDs. Therefore, the HEPES buffer (10 mM) with a pH value of 7.4 was chosen in the detection process. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of BPHA CDs remained unchanged as the ionic strength increased even when we increased the concentration of NaCl to 2.1 M (Fig. S3), which indicate the BPHA CDs possesses excellent optical stability and demonstrates a promising potential for physiological sensing applications.

CDs contain many carboxylic acid moieties at their surface, thus to adorn them with BPA might enhance the extent to coordinate with Cu2+, therefore enlarge the detecting range of Cu2+. As shown in Fig. 2 , upon increasing the dosage of Cu2+ to 2100 μM, the emission intensity decreases continuously, at which the fluorescence was quenched thoroughly and this indicated the BPHA CDs can detect Cu2+ in a wide range. The insert shows the plot of fluorescence quenching efficiency against the different concentration of Cu2+, which exhibits a relatively good linear. The limit of detection (LOD = 3σ/k, where σ is the standard variance of the blank determination, and k is the slope of the linear regression equation, respectively) of Cu2+ was calculated to be 0.094 μM. A plateau of the fluorescence quenching efficiency arrives over the 2100 μM of Cu2+, indicating the saturation of the chelating sites after Cu2+ binding. The quenching constant of Cu2+ was calculated as 8.30 × 103 L mol−1 based on the Stern-Volmer fitting of the titration plots. To the best of our knowledge, this is the widest detection range for Cu2+ so far. The results suggest that BPHA adorned can not only promote the coordination of Cu2+ but provide a possibility for a wide detection range of PPi. To avoid sediment at high concentration, 1.0 mM Cu2+ was chosen for the following quantitative determination of PPi.

To further evaluate the selectivity of this sensing system for Cu2+, we examined the fluorescence changes in the presence of the representative metal ions under the same conditions, including Na+, K+, Ca2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Ag+, Mg2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Mn2+ and NH4 +. The results are shown in Fig. 3 , where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the BPHA CDs at 450 nm in the absence and presence of the concerned ions. It is seen that a much higher fluorescence reduction value was observed upon addition of Cu2+. In contrast, no tremendous fluorescence quench was observed by addition of other ions into BPHA CDs dispersion (Fig. S6). These observations indicate a weak influence of other metal ions on our detecting system to sense Cu2+. The discrimination effect can be attributed to the particularly higher affinity of Cu2+ for the N, N-chelate groups of BPHA on the surface of CDs. As such, Cu2+ can quench the fluorescence of BPHA CDs via the electron or energy transfer [29].

To provide more clear evidence, in another series of experiments, selectivity studies of BPHA CDs towards Cu2+ over other cations were carried out by adding Cu2+ to a solution of BPHA CDs and each of the above 12 competing cations, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4 , the addition of Cu2+ to the mixture containing BPHA CDs and the competing cations dramatically decreased the fluorescent emission relative to free BPHA CDs in all cases. The high selectivity for Cu2+ over other cations, manifesting as decreased fluorescence, verify selectivity of BPHA CDs for Cu2+ in high concentration range.

To explore the feasibility of using the BPHA CDs/Cu2+ as the turn-on sensing probes for PPi, different dosage of PPi was added into 10 mM HEPES buffer (pH 7.4) containing BPHA CDs (15.52 μg mL−1) and Cu2+ (1.0 × 103 μM) to evaluate the response of BPHA CDs/Cu2+ toward PPi. As shown in Fig. 5 , after reacting with the increasing amounts of PPi, the fluorescence intensity of BPHA CDs/Cu2+ could be gradually recovered. Notably, ≈ 5.4% of the initial fluorescence intensity of the quenched BPHA CDs/Cu2+ could be recovered when the concentration of PPi was 1 μM, indicating the sensitivity of BPHA CDs/Cu2+ towards PPi. Moreover, when the concentration of the added PPi was up to 200 μM, ≈ 62% of the initial fluorescence intensity of the unquenched BPHA CDs could be recovered. A good linear fitting curve between fluorescence intensity ratio F/F0 (where F and F0 are the corresponding fluorescence intensities in the presence and absence of PPi, respectively) and concentration of PPi were obtained in the range of 2 × 10-6 M to 180 × 10-6 M with an R2 of 0.961 (the insert of Fig. 5). The limit of detection (LOD = 3σ/k, where σ is the standard variance of the blank determination, and k is the slope of the linear regression equation, respectively) of PPi was calculated to be 0.025 μM. The apparent association constant of BPHA CDs and Cu2+ was estimated to be 2.69 × 104 M−2 based on the Benesi-Hildebrand fitting method..

To evaluate the specificity of BPHA CDs/Cu2+ towards PPi, the fluorescence response of BPHA CDs/Cu2+ was examined in the presence of representative anions under the same conditions, including F–, Cl–, Br–, I–, CO3 2–, HCO3 –, NO3 –, BrO3 –, SO4 2–, SCN–, HPO4 2–, H2PO4 – and ATP. The results are shown in Fig. 6 , where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the BPHA CDs/Cu2+ at 450 nm in the absence and presence of the concerned anions, respectively. To be specific, a much higher fluorescence recovery was observed upon addition of PPi. In contrast, the fluorescence intensities of BPHA CDs/Cu2+ remained unchanged or slightly changed upon addition of the same concentration of the above anions. It is interesting that comparing with other anions, the addition of ATP can recovery more fluorescence intensity of BPHA CDs/Cu2+ (Fig. 6 and Fig. S7). DPA–Zn complexes have been reported to bind with ATP [51]. Similar with the DPA–Zn complexes, the selectivity of BPHA CDs/Cu2+ for PPi over ATP might be owed to the relatively smaller total charge of the four O–P oxygen atoms of ATP compared to those of PPi, which leads to reduced binding affinity. Therefore, in this scenario, ATP can recovery the fluorescence intensity to some extent but less than PPi.

To proceed further, we examined the competition effects of PPi and anions by adding PPi to a solution of BPHA CDs/Cu2+ and each of the above 13 competing anions, respectively. As shown in Fig. 7 , the addition of PPi to the mixtures containing BPHA CDs/Cu2+ and the competing anions increased the fluorescent emission dramatically relative to free BPHA CDs/Cu2+ in all cases except for Br– and BrO3 –, which is in accordance with the results of Fig. 6. All these results indicated that there was no remarkable interference to the analysis of PPi using BPHA CDs/Cu2+ system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection range and efficiency of PPi
ce:section.ce:para: The wide detection range of Cu2+ of BPHA CDs in turn prompted us to investigate the detection range and efficiency of PPi. As Fig. 8 and Fig. S8 show, with the increase of [Cu2+] to 2100 μM, 97% of the initial fluorescence intensity was quenched. When the concentrations of added PPi ranged from 500 to 4400 μM, more than 80% of the initial fluorescence intensity of the unquenched BPHA CDs could be recovered. Taken together, these data suggest that BPHA CDs could detect the widest range of PPi (4400 μM) by far.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence responses comparison of CDs and BPHA CDs
ce:section.ce:para: It is worthwhile mentioning that we aim at enlarging the detecting range of Cu2+ and PPi by enhancing the coordinate ability to Cu2+ with BPHA linkage. As shown in Fig. 9 and Fig. S9, with the addition of Cu2+, the fluorescence intensity decrease was estimated to be 62% for BPHA CDs and 32% for CDs, respectively. Thereafter, upon increasing dosage of PPi of the above quenched system, 72% and 52% of the initial fluorescence intensity BPHA CDs and CDs was recovered, respectively. Without Cu2+, PPi can hardly induce obvious fluorescence change of the BPHA CDs or CDs (green line). Copper ion has an unique property to quench the fluorescence by the paramagnetic effect. BPHA CDs is in quenching condition when copper ion combined with BPHA CDs, and after the addition of PPi, PPi would replace BPHA CDs, free BPHA CDs would release the fluorescent signal. But the replacement of PPi with BPHA is not completely, thus the fluorescence intensity of BPHA CDs + Cu2++PPi complex is lower than BPHA CDs. All these results indicated that BPHA linkage outside the CDs can not only promote the fluorescence quench extent by adding Cu2+ but the fluorescence recover extent by adding PPi of BPHA CDs in comparison with CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ppi binding performance on BPHA CDs by PV indicator
ce:section.ce:para: Changes in UV–vis spectra of PV with the increasing of BPHA CDs/Cu2+ are recorded in Fig. 10 . Upon addition of BPHA CDs/Cu2+ into the HEPES buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4) solution containing the indicator (PV 20 μM), the absorption peak of the ensembles at 444 nm decreased while the peaks at 627 nm increased, indicating the binding of PV to BPHA CDs/Cu2+.

Based on the alteration of absorbance intensities from titrations of BPHA CDs/Cu2+ to PV and PPi to BPHA CDs/Cu2+/PV, we obtained binding constant between BPHA CDs/Cu2+ and PV, which was calculated to be 3.06 × 104 M−1; constant between BPHA CDs/Cu2+/PV and PPi, which was calculated to be 5.70 × 106 M−1. In comparsion with the reported binding constant 5.75 × 106 M−1 between copper complex and PPi [50], BPHA CDs/Cu2+ shows an equivalent constant in PPi binding affinity.

The BPHA CDs/Cu2+/PV sensing system was applied for determination of PPi in human urine according to the procedures described in the experimental section. The results were listed in Table 1 . Using the standard addition method, the recoveries of spiked PPi ranged from 96.5% to 102.0% with RSDs lower than 4.4%. Moreover, it’s the first time to determine PPi concentration of different groups of people. The satisfactory results proved great potential for PPi detection in real biological samples.

In summary, a BPHA-modified CDs was fabricated for an effective detection of PPi in urine. The BPHA group not only improves the selectivity of BPHA CDs toward Cu2+ but also enlarge the linear-range for PPi detection. By IDAs in which pyrocatechol violet was used as the colorimetric indicator, BPHA CDs/Cu2+ was successfully applied to detect PPi with naked eyes. BPHA CDs provides new insight into the detection of PPi in biological diagnosis.

Yi Yang: Software. Pei Chen: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Yuqing Liu: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Zheng Cai: . Xiyao Wang: Writing - review & editing. Yang Me: Writing - original draft. Xiongyu Ding: Writing - original draft. Lan Lin: Writing - original draft. Huijun Jiang: Writing - review & editing. Zhenqin Zhang: Writing - review & editing. Yichun Ju: Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank Innovation and Entrepreurship Program of College Students in Jiangsu (No. KY109J2018049 and No 201910312087Y).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.119479.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Urolithiasis–an updated review over genetics, pathophysiology and its clinical management

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V. Sodimbaku, L. Pujari, Urolithiasis--an updated review over genetics, pathophysiology and its clinical management,. Int. J Pharm. Pharma. Sci. 6 (2014) 23–31.
